title: How to do middle age well
gist: A review of Netflix's Tina Fey series The Four Seasons, praising how it centres middle-aged adults themselves—omitting money, careers and children—to explore reinvention and friendship, and arguing it speaks to millennials, not just Gen X.

insights.claim: Netflix's The Four Seasons, created by and starring Tina Fey, follows three middle-aged couples across four holidays in a year, with its second season released at the end of May 2026.
insights.speaker: Imogen West-Knights
insights.so_what: A data point on Netflix commissioning ageing-focused comedy aimed at older demographics.

insights.claim: The show deliberately omits money, careers and children to focus on the adults themselves, making room for reinvention and unconventional configurations of 'family' in middle age.
insights.speaker: Imogen West-Knights
insights.so_what: Signals a narrative shift toward mid-life adult identity beyond parenthood in mainstream TV.
insights.locator: This is a show about all the things

insights.claim: Contrary to Vulture's Jen Chaney claiming the show won't appeal to under-30s, the reviewer argues it speaks specifically to millennials in their 30s who feel peers are 'locking down into closed-off units' of couples and children.
insights.speaker: Imogen West-Knights
insights.so_what: Suggests a broader cross-generational audience than the show's apparent Gen X target.
insights.locator: Back when the first season aired

insights.claim: The reviewer reads the series as a reminder that, beyond new parenthood, there is space and time for friendships to return to the core of a life.
insights.speaker: Imogen West-Knights
insights.so_what: Captures a cultural appetite for stories foregrounding adult friendship over family units.
insights.locator: This show is a refreshing reminder
